refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable end-to-end teleoperation communication with latency that permits safe remote operation.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting teleoperation capability, and (2) ensuring latency is compatible with safety. The refined version separates the capability requirement from the performance constraint. The phrase 'compatible with safe remote operation' remains in the FR as it defines the functional intent (what safety-critical latency means in context), while the specific acceptance criterion (p95 ≤ 150 ms) belongs in the constraint/acceptance criteria layer, not the FR itself. This keeps the FR solution-neutral and problem-focused.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure end-to-end latency (sensor input to actuator response) under agreed network conditions. Confirm that measured p95 latency meets the acceptance criterion of ≤150 ms. Validate through controlled teleoperation trials with Safety Manager and Tele-operator that latency permits safe command execution and hazard response.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The NGHE solution shall transmit operator control inputs from the remote station to the equipment.
FR-NGHE-008.2: The NGHE solution shall transmit equipment state and sensor feedback from the equipment to the remote station.
FR-NGHE-008.3: The NGHE solution shall execute operator commands on the equipment with deterministic response timing.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement a deterministic, low-latency communication pipeline with end-to-end latency budgeting across acquisition, transmission, processing, and actuation stages, using priority-queued message handling and bounded processing windows to meet p95 ≤ 150 ms.

DP2: Deploy a redundant dual-path communication architecture (primary + failover) with automatic switchover logic and synchronized state management to ensure safety-critical command delivery within latency bounds even under partial network degradation.

DP3: Establish a hierarchical control model with local autonomous safety enforcement at the remote site (independent of uplink latency) and supervisory teleoperation commands from the operator, decoupling operator reaction time from safety-critical response time.
DP4: Implement adaptive bitrate and compression strategies with predictive buffering to maintain consistent latency under variable network conditions while preserving command fidelity.

DP5: Use a time-synchronized, event-driven architecture with explicit latency instrumentation and closed-loop feedback to continuously monitor and adapt communication parameters to maintain p95 ≤ 150 ms.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP3 (Hierarchical Control Model) + DP1 (Deterministic Low-Latency Pipeline) as integrated solution: Decouple operator reaction latency from safety-critical response by embedding autonomous safety enforcement at the remote site (independent of uplink), while the deterministic pipeline ensures supervisory commands and telemetry meet p95 ≤ 150 ms. This avoids false coupling between operator cognitive latency and system latency, and provides defense-in-depth.

potential_sources: DP1 + DP2 coupling: Redundant failover logic (DP2) may introduce additional latency variance if switchover detection and state reconciliation are not carefully bounded; recommend explicit timeout and state-sync budgets.

DP3 + DP4 coupling: Adaptive bitrate (DP4) may conflict with deterministic latency (DP1) if compression/decompression introduces variable delays; recommend fixed-latency codec or pre-computed compression tables.

DP3 + worksite safety plan coupling: Autonomous safety enforcement (DP3) must be validated against worksite operational policies; if policies require operator override capability, this creates a secondary control path that may re-introduce latency coupling.
DP1 + network variability: Deterministic pipeline assumes bounded network jitter; if network conditions are highly variable, p95 budget may be insufficient without DP2 or DP4 adaptation.
coupling_check.notes: DP3 is the strongest logical decoupler because it removes the false dependency on operator reaction time. DP1 provides the necessary determinism. DP2 and DP4 are secondary risk mitigations. The integrated design is loosely coupled if state reconciliation (DP2) and codec selection (DP4) are treated as orthogonal concerns with explicit latency budgets.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: 'Agreed network conditions' are stable and characterized (e.g., jitter distribution, packet loss rate, bandwidth). RISK: If network conditions are not formally specified or vary significantly, p95 ≤ 150 ms may be unachievable; recommend network SLA definition before DP realization.

ASSUMPTION: Autonomous safety enforcement at remote site can be implemented independently of operator commands. RISK: If worksite policies require real-time operator approval for all safety-critical actions, DP3 decoupling is negated; recommend policy review.

ASSUMPTION: Telemetry feedback (video, sensor data) is not latency-critical for safety. RISK: If operator situational awareness depends on real-time video, latency in telemetry path may indirectly affect operator reaction time and safety; recommend explicit telemetry latency budget.

ASSUMPTION: 'Safe remote operation' is defined by worksite safety plan. RISK: If safety criteria are ambiguous or conflict with latency targets, DP selection may be invalid; recommend safety requirements traceability.

ASSUMPTION: End-to-end latency includes only command path (operator → remote site). RISK: If telemetry feedback loop is included in latency budget, p95 ≤ 150 ms may be insufficient for round-trip control; recommend clarification of latency scope.

ASSUMPTION: Deterministic latency can be achieved without sacrificing throughput or command expressiveness. RISK: Bounded processing windows may limit command complexity or frequency; recommend latency-throughput trade-off analysis.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): The NGHE solution shall enable end-to-end teleoperation communication with latency that permits safe remote operation, where (1) command latency (operator input → remote actuation) shall not exceed p95 ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions, (2) autonomous safety enforcement at the remote site shall be independent of operator latency and shall enforce worksite safety policies without uplink dependency, and (3) telemetry feedback (video, sensor data) latency shall be characterized separately and shall not degrade operator situational awareness below worksite safety plan thresholds. Acceptance criteria: (a) p95 command latency ≤ 150 ms measured end-to-end under specified network SLA, (b) autonomous safety enforcement response time ≤ [TBD] ms independent of operator input, (c) telemetry feedback latency ≤ [TBD] ms, (d) failover switchover time ≤ [TBD] ms with zero command loss, (e) worksite safety plan compliance verified by [safety authority]. This revision clarifies scope, decouples safety from operator latency, and makes acceptance criteria measurable.

im_boundary.name: Safety Services
im_boundary.scope: Autonomous safety enforcement logic, state machines, and decision rules that operate independently of uplink connectivity. Includes local hazard detection, constraint enforcement, and safe-state transitions. Does NOT include operator interface, telemetry aggregation, or network transport.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives sensor data and vehicle state from Vehicle module to inform local safety decisions (e.g., position, velocity, obstacle proximity).

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Emits safety violations, constraint breaches, and autonomous corrective actions to Vehicle and Teleoperation modules.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands from Teleoperation; Safety Services validates against local constraints before forwarding to Vehicle execution.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Monitors uplink health; triggers failsafe modes if connectivity is lost or degraded beyond acceptable thresholds.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. DP3 (Hierarchical Control Model) autonomous enforcement and DP1 (Deterministic Low-Latency Pipeline) safety-critical response logic are naturally owned here. Local decision-making, constraint enforcement, and failsafe state machines are core to this module's charter. Minor diffusion: SafetyAlert must propagate to Vehicle for actuation and Teleoperation for operator awareness, but this is expected boundary crossing, not diffusion of core logic.

Poor fit. Vehicle module owns actuation and low-level control, not safety policy. Embedding DP3 (hierarchical safety model) here would couple safety logic to vehicle-specific hardware, making it non-reusable and difficult to update. DP1 (deterministic pipeline) would also diffuse across Vehicle and Teleoperation, violating separation of concerns.

Poor fit. Teleoperation owns operator interface and command translation, not autonomous safety enforcement. Placing DP3 here would entangle operator cognitive latency with safety response, exactly the false coupling the DP is designed to avoid. DP1 deterministic pipeline would also diffuse across network and vehicle layers.

Moderate fit but too broad. NGHE Solution is a system-level container. While it could logically house Safety Services, using it as the IM boundary obscures the actual ownership and accountability for safety logic. Prefer the more specific Safety Services module to maintain clarity and enable independent evolution.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Safety Services is the natural owner of both DP3 (Hierarchical Control Model with autonomous enforcement) and DP1 (Deterministic Low-Latency Pipeline for safety-critical response). The module's charter is to enforce safety constraints independently of uplink and operator latency. By centralizing autonomous safety logic here, we achieve high cohesion (all safety decisions in one place), minimize diffusion (SafetyAlert and Tasking validation are expected cross-module interfaces, not logic leakage), and enable defense-in-depth without false coupling between operator reaction time and system safety response.
containment_risks: Risk: SafetyAlert interface may become a bottleneck if safety decisions must propagate to both Vehicle (for immediate actuation) and Teleoperation (for operator notification) synchronously. Mitigation: Design SafetyAlert as a non-blocking publish-subscribe interface; Vehicle and Teleoperation consume asynchronously.

Risk: Telemetry input dependency on Vehicle module creates a coupling path. If Vehicle telemetry is delayed or lossy, Safety Services decisions may be based on stale state. Mitigation: Implement local state estimation and timeout-based failsafe in Safety Services; do not rely solely on Vehicle telemetry for critical decisions.

Risk: Network Link monitoring interface may require tight coupling to network stack. If network health signals are not cleanly abstracted, Safety Services becomes entangled with transport details. Mitigation: Define Network Link as a simple boolean or enum (e.g., CONNECTED, DEGRADED, LOST); hide transport complexity in Teleoperation or a dedicated network abstraction layer.

Risk: Tasking validation logic in Safety Services may grow to include domain-specific command semantics, diffusing into Vehicle or Teleoperation concerns. Mitigation: Keep Safety Services focused on constraint enforcement (e.g., 'reject if velocity > max_safe_speed'); delegate command parsing and sequencing to Teleoperation.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: HIGH. The FR requirement ('enable end-to-end teleoperation communication with latency that permits safe remote operation') is directly traceable to the Safety Services IM boundary. Acceptance criterion p95 ≤ 150 ms is measurable at the Teleoperation module's command-to-acknowledgment path. Safety enforcement (autonomous constraint validation) is verifiable through unit tests of state machines and failsafe logic within Safety Services. Network Link monitoring and Telemetry input latencies are instrumentable. Worksite safety plan compliance is enforceable through Safety Services' constraint rules (e.g., geofencing, velocity limits, obstacle avoidance thresholds).
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: CLEAR. The FR is decomposed into two orthogonal concerns: (1) latency guarantee (owned by DP1 deterministic pipeline, realized in Safety Services + Teleoperation), and (2) safety enforcement (owned by DP3 hierarchical control, realized in Safety Services). The allocation avoids false coupling: operator cognitive latency does not degrade system safety response because autonomous enforcement is independent of uplink. Safety Services is the single point of accountability for both concerns, eliminating ambiguity about who owns safety-critical decisions.

diffusion_risks: MODERATE: SafetyAlert interface propagates to both Vehicle (actuation) and Teleoperation (operator notification). If SafetyAlert emission becomes synchronous or blocking, it creates a latency bottleneck that violates p95 ≤ 150 ms. Mitigation: Enforce non-blocking publish-subscribe; measure SafetyAlert emission latency as part of p95 acceptance test.

MODERATE: Telemetry input dependency on Vehicle module introduces a coupling path. Stale or lossy telemetry from Vehicle could cause Safety Services to make decisions on outdated state (e.g., obstacle proximity). This is a data freshness risk, not a logic diffusion risk, but it undermines the independence claim of DP3. Mitigation: Implement local state estimation (e.g., Kalman filter) and timeout-based failsafe in Safety Services; define maximum acceptable telemetry age; reject stale data and transition to safe state if freshness threshold is exceeded.

LOW: Network Link monitoring interface risks tight coupling to transport layer. If network health signals are not cleanly abstracted (e.g., raw socket errors instead of CONNECTED/DEGRADED/LOST enum), Safety Services becomes entangled with Teleoperation's network stack. Mitigation: Define Network Link as a simple, transport-agnostic interface; hide complexity in Teleoperation or a dedicated network abstraction layer.

LOW: Tasking validation logic may creep to include domain-specific command semantics (e.g., 'reject if command sequence violates operational policy'). This risks diffusing Vehicle or Teleoperation concerns into Safety Services. Mitigation: Scope Tasking validation strictly to constraint enforcement (position, velocity, acceleration bounds); delegate command parsing, sequencing, and policy interpretation to Teleoperation.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: EXCELLENT CONTAINMENT (0.92 score justified). DP3 (Hierarchical Control Model) and DP1 (Deterministic Low-Latency Pipeline) are naturally co-located in Safety Services because both require autonomous, low-latency decision-making independent of uplink. The module's charter—'enforce safety constraints independently of uplink and operator latency'—directly maps to both DPs. Cross-module interfaces (Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Tasking, Network Link) are expected boundary crossings, not logic leakage. The four identified containment risks are all mitigatable through interface design and local failsafe logic, not architectural restructuring. No evidence of DP diffusion across Vehicle or Teleoperation modules.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP3 + DP1 integration directly addresses the FR. DP3 (Hierarchical Control Model with autonomous enforcement) solves the safety aspect ('permits safe remote operation') by decoupling operator latency from safety response. DP1 (Deterministic Low-Latency Pipeline) solves the latency aspect ('latency that permits safe remote operation') by guaranteeing p95 ≤ 150 ms for supervisory commands and telemetry. The integration avoids false coupling: operator cognitive latency (typically 200–500 ms) does not block safety-critical response (autonomous, <50 ms). This is a sound design principle. The DP is complete, non-redundant, and independent: neither DP alone solves the FR; together they provide defense-in-depth. Acceptance criterion p95 ≤ 150 ms is directly measurable from DP1's deterministic pipeline implementation.

recommended_revisions: REVISION 1: Formalize the Telemetry input contract. Define maximum acceptable age (e.g., 100 ms) and staleness handling (e.g., failsafe to safe state if Telemetry is not received within timeout). Add this to Safety Services' interface specification and acceptance tests.

REVISION 2: Specify SafetyAlert as non-blocking, publish-subscribe. Define latency budget for SafetyAlert emission (e.g., <10 ms from decision to publish) and measure as part of p95 acceptance test. Document that Vehicle and Teleoperation consume asynchronously.

REVISION 3: Abstract Network Link monitoring as a simple enum (CONNECTED, DEGRADED, LOST) with clear thresholds (e.g., DEGRADED if packet loss >5% or RTT >100 ms; LOST if RTT >200 ms or no heartbeat for 500 ms). Hide transport-layer details in Teleoperation or a dedicated network abstraction layer.

REVISION 4: Scope Tasking validation strictly to constraint enforcement (position bounds, velocity limits, acceleration limits, geofencing). Document that command parsing, sequencing, and operational policy interpretation remain in Teleoperation. Add a design rule: 'Safety Services shall not interpret command semantics; it shall only enforce constraints on command parameters.'

REVISION 5: Add a local state estimation module (or sub-component) within Safety Services to reduce dependency on Vehicle telemetry freshness. This improves robustness of autonomous enforcement and reduces coupling to Vehicle module's telemetry latency.

REVISION 6: Document the failsafe state machine explicitly. Define transitions for each Network Link state (CONNECTED → normal operation; DEGRADED → reduced autonomy or operator confirmation required; LOST → safe state). Include timeout-based transitions (e.g., if CONNECTED for >5 s without Telemetry, transition to DEGRADED).

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-IM, DP-IM, FR-DP) pass with high confidence, and containment is excellent (0.92). However, the gate results surface six critical mitigations that must be formalized before proceeding to sub-FR decomposition. These are not architectural flaws but rather interface contracts and failsafe logic that are essential to the safety-critical nature of this system. Revising the current level to incorporate these mitigations ensures that the design is robust and that sub-level decomposition inherits a well-specified, low-ambiguity parent. Given high safety criticality, deferring these clarifications to lower levels risks propagating ambiguity into implementation.

revise.why: The DP (DP1 + DP3 integration) is logically sound, but its realization contract must be tightened. Specifically: (1) DP1's deterministic pipeline must explicitly include latency budgets for SafetyAlert emission and Telemetry input staleness detection; (2) DP3's hierarchical control must formalize the failsafe state machine and local state estimation to reduce coupling to Vehicle telemetry; (3) Network Link abstraction must be specified as a simple, transport-agnostic interface. These revisions do not change the DP logic but make its implementation constraints explicit and measurable. Revise the DP specification document to include: (a) Telemetry input contract (max age 100 ms, staleness handling), (b) SafetyAlert emission latency budget (<10 ms), (c) Network Link enum and thresholds, (d) Tasking validation scope (constraint enforcement only, no command semantics), (e) Local state estimation sub-component within Safety Services, (f) Failsafe state machine with explicit transitions and timeouts.

assumptions: Telemetry from Vehicle module is available at ≥10 Hz (100 ms period); Safety Services can tolerate up to 100 ms staleness before failsafe triggers.
SafetyAlert publish-subscribe mechanism is non-blocking and can emit within <10 ms from decision to publish.
Network Link monitoring is abstracted as a simple enum (CONNECTED, DEGRADED, LOST) with clear thresholds; transport-layer details are hidden in Teleoperation or a dedicated network abstraction layer.
Tasking validation is scoped strictly to constraint enforcement (position, velocity, acceleration bounds, geofencing); command parsing and operational policy interpretation remain in Teleoperation.
Local state estimation (e.g., Kalman filter) within Safety Services can reduce dependency on Vehicle telemetry freshness and improve robustness of autonomous enforcement.
Failsafe state machine is deterministic and can transition to safe state within <50 ms in response to Network Link LOST or Telemetry timeout.
open_risks: MODERATE: SafetyAlert interface propagates to both Vehicle and Teleoperation. If emission becomes synchronous or blocking, latency budget is violated. Mitigation: Enforce non-blocking publish-subscribe; measure SafetyAlert emission latency as part of p95 acceptance test.

MODERATE: Telemetry input dependency on Vehicle module introduces data freshness risk. Stale telemetry could cause Safety Services to make decisions on outdated state. Mitigation: Implement local state estimation and timeout-based failsafe; define maximum acceptable telemetry age (100 ms); reject stale data and transition to safe state if freshness threshold is exceeded.

LOW: Network Link monitoring interface risks tight coupling to transport layer. If network health signals are not cleanly abstracted, Safety Services becomes entangled with Teleoperation's network stack. Mitigation: Define Network Link as a simple, transport-agnostic interface; hide complexity in Teleoperation or a dedicated network abstraction layer.

LOW: Tasking validation logic may creep to include domain-specific command semantics. This risks diffusing Vehicle or Teleoperation concerns into Safety Services. Mitigation: Scope Tasking validation strictly to constraint enforcement; delegate command parsing, sequencing, and policy interpretation to Teleoperation.

UNQUANTIFIED: Failsafe state machine transitions (CONNECTED → DEGRADED → LOST) must be deterministic and fast (<50 ms). If transitions are delayed or non-deterministic, safety response is compromised. Mitigation: Formalize state machine with explicit timeouts and transition rules; verify through model checking or simulation.
evidence_needed: Formal specification of Telemetry input contract: maximum acceptable age (100 ms), staleness detection mechanism, failsafe transition rule.
Latency budget for SafetyAlert emission: <10 ms from decision to publish; measurement methodology and acceptance test.

Network Link abstraction specification: enum definition (CONNECTED, DEGRADED, LOST), thresholds (e.g., DEGRADED if packet loss >5% or RTT >100 ms; LOST if RTT >200 ms or no heartbeat for 500 ms), and interface contract.

Tasking validation scope document: explicit list of constraints enforced (position bounds, velocity limits, acceleration limits, geofencing); explicit list of concerns delegated to Teleoperation (command parsing, sequencing, operational policy).
Local state estimation design: algorithm selection (e.g., Kalman filter), update frequency, accuracy requirements, and integration with failsafe logic.
Failsafe state machine specification: state diagram, transition rules, timeout values, and latency guarantees for each transition.

Integration test plan: end-to-end latency measurement (p95 ≤ 150 ms) under nominal and degraded network conditions; SafetyAlert emission latency measurement; Telemetry staleness detection and failsafe transition verification.

Safety case evidence: verification that autonomous enforcement (DP3) is independent of uplink latency; verification that failsafe state machine is deterministic and safe under all network failure modes.
